content:
# Exodus Chapter 22 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~45v–46r (PDF pp. 88–89); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 64–65 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_022_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_022_page_002.jpg`

---

### Theft and Restitution — The Cost of Taking What Is Not Yours (vv. 1–4)

**1** "If someone steals an ox or a sheep and slaughters it or sells it, the thief must pay back five head of cattle for the ox and four sheep for the one sheep.

**2** "If a thief is caught breaking in and is struck and killed, there is no bloodguilt for the killer. **3** But if the sun has risen on him, there is bloodguilt. The thief must make full restitution. If he has nothing, he is to be sold to pay for what he stole. **4** If the stolen animal is actually found alive in his possession — whether ox, donkey, or sheep — he must pay back double.

---

### Crop and Field Damage — Responsibility for What You Let Loose (vv. 5–6)

**5** "If someone lets livestock graze in a field or vineyard and turns them loose so that they feed in another person's field, restitution must be made from the best of his own field and the best of his own vineyard.

**6** "If a fire breaks out and catches in thornbushes, and stacked grain or standing grain or the whole field is consumed, the one who started the fire must make full restitution.

---

### Laws About Property Held in Trust (vv. 7–13)

**7** "If someone gives a neighbor silver or goods for safekeeping and they are stolen from that person's house — if the thief is caught, the thief pays double. **8** But if the thief is not found, the owner of the house must come before God to determine whether he himself laid hands on the neighbor's property.

**9** "In every case of disputed ownership — whether over an ox, a donkey, a sheep, a garment, or any lost item about which someone says, 'This is mine' — both parties are to bring their case before God. The one whom God declares guilty must pay double to the other.

**10** "If someone gives a donkey, an ox, a sheep, or any animal to a neighbor for safekeeping and it dies, is injured, or is driven off while no one is watching, **11** the matter between them shall be settled by an oath before YHWH: that the keeper did not lay hands on the other's property. The owner must accept this, and no restitution is required. **12** But if the animal was actually stolen from the keeper, restitution must be made to the owner. **13** If it was torn apart by a wild beast, the keeper shall bring the remains as evidence. No restitution is required for what was torn apart.

---

### Borrowed Property — When You Hold What Belongs to Another (vv. 14–15)

**14** "If someone borrows an animal from a neighbor and it is injured or dies while the owner is not present, full restitution must be made. **15** If the owner was present, no restitution is required. If it was hired, the loss is covered by the hiring fee.

---

### Seduction of an Unbetrothed Woman (vv. 16–17)

**16** "If a man seduces a young woman who is not betrothed and sleeps with her, he must pay the bride-price in full and take her as his wife. **17** If her father absolutely refuses to give her to him, the man must still pay the amount equal to the bride-price for young women.

---

### Offenses Worthy of Death — Sorcery, Bestiality, and Idolatry (vv. 18–20)

**18** "You must not allow a sorceress to live.

**19** "Anyone who lies with an animal must be put to death.

**20** "Anyone who sacrifices to any god other than YHWH alone must be devoted to destruction.

---

### The Heart of the Covenant — Protecting the Vulnerable (vv. 21–27)

**21** "You must not mistreat a stranger or oppress him, for you yourselves were strangers in the land of Egypt. **22** You must not take advantage of any widow or fatherless child. **23** If you do take advantage of them and they cry out to me, I will certainly hear their cry. **24** My anger will burn, and I will kill you with the sword, so that your own wives become widows and your own children become fatherless.

**25** "If you lend money to any of my people — to the poor among you — you must not act like a creditor toward them. You must not charge them interest. **26** If you take your neighbor's cloak as collateral, you must return it to them before the sun goes down, **27** because that cloak is the only covering they have for their body. What else will they sleep in? And when they cry out to me, I will hear — because I am compassionate.

---

### Reverence, Firstfruits, and Consecration (vv. 28–31)

**28** "You must not revile God, and you must not curse a leader of your people.

**29** "You must not hold back the fullness of your harvest or the overflow of your presses. The firstborn of your sons you must give to me. **30** You must do the same with your cattle and your sheep: for seven days the firstborn may remain with its mother, but on the eighth day you must give it to me.

**31** "You are to be people set apart for me. You must not eat the flesh of an animal torn apart in the field. Throw it to the dogs."

---

## Meaning Notes

**v. 1 — FIVEFOLD AND FOURFOLD RESTITUTION: THE ECONOMICS OF THEFT (Heb. *chamishah vaqar yeshallem tachat hashshor ve'arba tson tachat hasseh*, חֲמִשָּׁ֣ה בָקָר֩ יְשַׁלֵּ֨ם תַּ֣חַת הַשּׁ֔וֹר וְאַרְבַּע־צֹ֕אן תַּ֖חַת הַשֶּֽׂה):** The asymmetric penalty — five cattle for an ox, four sheep for a sheep — reflects not arbitrary numbers but economic logic. An ox (*shor*, שׁוֹר) is a working animal, a means of production; its theft removes from the victim not only the animal but the capacity to plow and earn a livelihood. The higher penalty compensates for this compounding loss. A sheep (*seh*, שֶׂה) is valuable but does not represent the same loss of productive capacity. The restitution is calibrated to the actual harm, not merely the market value of what was taken — a remarkably sophisticated principle. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders this faithfully with *pente moschous* (πέντε μόσχους) and *tessara probata* (τέσσαρα πρόβατα), preserving the ratio precisely.

**vv. 2–3 — THE THIEF IN THE DARK: JUSTIFIABLE AND UNJUSTIFIABLE KILLING (Heb. *im-bamachteret yimmatse haggannav*, אִם־בַּמַּחְתֶּ֛רֶת יִמָּצֵ֥א הַגַּנָּ֖ב):** The word *machteret* (מַחְתֶּרֶת, "tunnel, breach") refers to a thief breaking through the mud-brick wall of a house — a violent act that implies the potential for confrontation. At night, the homeowner cannot know whether the intruder intends theft or murder, so lethal force is permitted: *ein lo damim* (אֵ֥ין ל֖וֹ דָּמִֽים, literally "there are no bloods for him"), meaning no bloodguilt attaches. But once the sun has risen (*im-zarecha hashshemesh alav*, אִם־זָרְחָ֥ה הַשֶּׁ֛מֶשׁ עָלָ֖יו), the situation changes — in daylight, the homeowner can identify the intruder and seek legal recourse. Killing a daytime thief is therefore murder. The LXX adds a clarifying detail absent from the Hebrew, specifying the thief is struck and dies (*kai apothane*), making the scenario explicit. This is one of the earliest legal distinctions between self-defense and excessive force.

**vv. 7–9 — "BEFORE GOD": OATH AND ADJUDICATION (Heb. *veniqrav ba'al-habbayit el-ha'elohim*, וְנִקְרַ֥ב בַּֽעַל־הַבַּ֖יִת אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִ֑ים):** The phrase *el-ha'elohim* (אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִים, "to God/the gods/the judges") is one of the most debated in Exodus. It may mean (1) to God himself, indicating a sacral oath at the sanctuary; (2) to the judges, who serve as God's representatives (cf. Psalm 82:1, "God takes his stand in the divine assembly"); or (3) to a divine ordeal or oracle. The LXX consistently translates *enantios tou theou* (ἐνώπιον τοῦ θεοῦ, "before God"), favoring the theological reading. What matters theologically is the principle: when human evidence fails, the case is not simply dropped — it is elevated to a higher court. The covenant community does not tolerate unresolved injustice. Even when proof is impossible, the oath before God places the dispute under divine scrutiny, and the false swearer will answer to YHWH himself.

**vv. 10–13 — BAILMENT LAW: THE OATH AS LEGAL INSTRUMENT (Heb. *shevuat YHWH tihyeh bein sheneihem*, שְׁבֻעַ֣ת יְהוָ֗ה תִּהְיֶה֙ בֵּ֣ין שְׁנֵיהֶ֔ם):** The "oath of YHWH" (*shevuat YHWH*) is not a casual promise but a formal legal instrument that transfers liability from the human to the divine realm. The keeper who swears that he did not misappropriate the entrusted animal places himself under YHWH's judgment. The owner must accept this (*velaqach be'alav*, וְלָקַ֥ח בְּעָלָ֖יו, "and the owner shall accept it") — the oath is legally conclusive. But three categories of loss are distinguished: (1) natural death or injury with no witnesses — resolved by oath; (2) theft — the keeper is liable because theft implies negligence in guarding; (3) torn by a wild animal — the keeper is exonerated upon producing the torn remains (*ha-terefah*, הַטְּרֵפָ֖ה) as evidence. The LXX preserves this tripartite structure with precision.

**vv. 16–17 — SEDUCTION AND THE BRIDE-PRICE (Heb. *mohar yimharenah lo le'ishah*, מָהֹ֛ר יִמְהָרֶ֥נָּה לּ֖וֹ לְאִשָּֽׁה):** The *mohar* (מֹ֫הַר, "bride-price") is not a purchase price for the woman but a compensation to her family for the economic and social loss of a daughter. The repetition of the root — *mohar yimharenah* — emphasizes certainty: "he shall surely pay the bride-price." The father retains the right to refuse the marriage (v. 17), but the financial obligation stands regardless. This law protects the woman's social standing: a seduced but unmarried woman in the ancient world faced devastating social consequences. The law ensures that the man who created this situation bears the cost, whether or not the marriage occurs. The LXX uses *pherne* (φερνή) for the bride-price, a term from Greek marriage custom, adapting the cultural concept for its audience.

**v. 18 — THE SORCERESS (Heb. *mekhashshefah lo techayeh*, מְכַשֵּׁפָ֖ה לֹ֥א תְחַיֶּֽה):** The feminine form *mekhashshefah* (מְכַשֵּׁפָה) does not restrict this law to women; it reflects the social reality that sorcery was primarily associated with women in the ancient Near East. The verb *kishshef* (כִּשֵּׁף) refers to the use of spells, potions, or rituals to manipulate reality apart from YHWH — an act of practical atheism that denies YHWH's sovereignty over creation. The LXX uses *pharmakous* (φαρμακούς, "sorcerers/poisoners"), shifting to a masculine plural and reflecting the Greek association of sorcery with drug preparation (*pharmakon*, φάρμακον). The prohibition is absolute: sorcery is incompatible with covenant life because it seeks power from sources other than the God who has revealed himself.

**v. 20 — DEVOTED TO DESTRUCTION (Heb. *zoveach la'elohim yochoram bilti l'YHWH levaddo*, זֹבֵ֣חַ לָאֱלֹהִ֔ים יָחֳרָ֑ם בִּלְתִּ֥י לַיהוָ֖ה לְבַדּֽוֹ):** The verb *yochoram* (יָחֳרָם) is from *charam* (חרם), meaning "to devote to destruction" — the same root used for the ban (*cherem*) in holy war. The idolater is placed under the *cherem*: removed entirely from the community and from life. The qualifying phrase *bilti l'YHWH levaddo* (בִּלְתִּי לַיהוָה לְבַדּוֹ, "except to YHWH alone") is the exclusive claim of the first commandment expressed in legal consequence. The LXX renders *yochoram* as *olethreuthesontai* (ὀλεθρευθήσονται, "they shall be utterly destroyed"), capturing the totality of the sentence.

**vv. 21–24 — THE STRANGER, THE WIDOW, THE ORPHAN: THE TRIAD OF VULNERABILITY (Heb. *ger lo-toneh velo tilchatsenu ki-gerim heyitem be'erets Mitsrayim*, גֵּ֥ר לֹא־תוֹנֶ֖ה וְלֹ֣א תִלְחָצֶ֑נּוּ כִּֽי־גֵרִ֥ים הֱיִיתֶ֖ם בְּאֶ֥רֶץ מִצְרָֽיִם):** Two distinct verbs are used for mistreatment of the stranger: *toneh* (תוֹנֶה, from *yanah*, "to wrong, oppress, take advantage of" — especially in financial dealings) and *tilchatsenu* (תִלְחָצֶנּוּ, from *lachats*, "to press, squeeze, crush" — physical or economic pressure). Together they cover the full spectrum of exploitation. The motivation clause — *ki-gerim heyitem* ("for you were strangers") — is the most distinctive feature of Israelite ethics: moral obligation is grounded not in abstract principle but in lived experience. You know what it feels like, therefore you must not inflict it.

The escalation in vv. 22–24 is devastating in its rhetorical precision. If Israel oppresses widows and orphans, YHWH will respond in kind: "your wives will become widows and your children fatherless" (*vehayu nesheikhem almanot uveneikhem yetomim*, וְהָי֥וּ נְשֵׁיכֶ֛ם אַלְמָנ֖וֹת וּבְנֵיכֶ֥ם יְתֹמִֽים). The punishment mirrors the crime with terrifying exactness. The verb *tsa'oq* (צָעַק, "to cry out") echoes Israel's own cry in Egypt (Exod. 2:23; 3:7) — the same verb, the same God, the same response. YHWH's compassion is not selective: if Israel becomes Egypt, YHWH will treat Israel as he treated Egypt.

**vv. 25–27 — THE CLOAK, THE COLD, AND THE CHARACTER OF GOD (Heb. *ki hi khesu-to levaddah hi simlato le'oro bammeh yishkav*, כִּ֣י הִ֤וא כְסוּתוֹ֙ לְבַדָּ֔הּ הִ֥וא שִׂמְלָת֖וֹ לְעֹר֑וֹ בַּמֶּ֣ה יִשְׁכָּ֔ב):** This is one of the most poignant passages in all of biblical law. The creditor holds the debtor's *salmah* (שַׂלְמָה, "outer cloak, mantle") — the large garment that served as both clothing by day and blanket by night. The law does not prohibit taking the cloak as collateral; it requires returning it before sunset. The rhetorical question — *bammeh yishkav* (בַּמֶּה יִשְׁכָּב, "in what will he sleep?") — forces the creditor to see the debtor as a human being, not merely a financial obligation.

The final clause is extraordinary: *vehayyah ki-yits'aq elai veshama'ti ki-channun ani* (וְהָיָ֥ה כִּֽי־יִצְעַ֖ק אֵלַ֑י וְשָׁמַעְתִּ֖י כִּֽי־חַנּ֥וּן אָֽנִי, "and when he cries out to me, I will hear — because I am compassionate"). The word *channun* (חַנּוּן, "gracious, compassionate") is one of YHWH's core self-descriptions (cf. Exod. 34:6). The entire legal system rests finally on divine character: YHWH hears the cry of the shivering poor person not because of a legal technicality but because compassion is who YHWH is. The LXX translates *channun* as *eleemon* (ἐλεήμων, "merciful"), connecting this passage to the broader Greek biblical vocabulary of divine mercy. Law and mercy are not opposites; in YHWH's covenant, law is the instrument of mercy.

**v. 28 — GOD AND THE LEADER (Heb. *Elohim lo teqallel venasi ve'ammekha lo ta'or*, אֱלֹהִ֖ים לֹ֣א תְקַלֵּ֑ל וְנָשִׂ֥יא בְעַמְּךָ֖ לֹ֥א תָאֹֽר):** Two different words for "curse" are used: *teqallel* (תְקַלֵּל, from *qalal*, "to make light of, treat with contempt") for God, and *ta'or* (תָאֹר, from *'arar*, "to curse, invoke harm upon") for the leader (*nasi*, נָשִׂיא, "one who is lifted up, chief, prince"). The pairing of God and human leader in a single prohibition establishes that legitimate authority, though human, participates in the divine order. To curse the leader is not merely a political offense but a violation of the covenant's ordering of community life.

**vv. 29–30 — FIRSTFRUITS AND FIRSTBORN: THE PRINCIPLE OF THE FIRST PORTION (Heb. *male'atkha vedim'akha lo te'acher bekhor banekha titten-li*, מְלֵאָתְךָ֥ וְדִמְעֲךָ֖ לֹ֣א תְאַחֵ֑ר בְּכ֥וֹר בָּנֶ֖יךָ תִּתֶּן־לִּֽי):** The word *male'ah* (מְלֵאָה, "fullness") refers to the ripe grain harvest, and *dim'ah* (דִּמְעָה, literally "tear, dripping") is the overflow of the wine or olive press — a beautifully concrete metaphor for abundance. The command not to "delay" (*te'acher*, from *'achar*, אחר) establishes the principle of priority: the first and best goes to YHWH, not the leftovers. This is connected immediately to the firstborn of sons and animals, creating a single theological principle: everything that opens life belongs to the One who gives life.

**v. 31 — HOLY PEOPLE, TORN FLESH (Heb. *ve'anshei-qodesh tihyun li uvasar bassadeh terefah lo tokhelu lakkelev tashlikhun oto*, וְאַנְשֵׁי־קֹ֖דֶשׁ תִּהְי֣וּן לִ֑י וּבָשָׂ֨ר בַּשָּׂדֶ֤ה טְרֵפָה֙ לֹ֣א תֹאכֵ֔לוּ לַכֶּ֖לֶב תַּשְׁלִכ֥וּן אֹתֽוֹ):** The chapter's final verse reframes everything that preceded it. The phrase *anshei-qodesh* (אַנְשֵׁי־קֹדֶשׁ, "people of holiness") is striking — not *am qadosh* ("holy nation") as in 19:6, but literally "men of holiness," emphasizing that holiness is not merely a national status but an individual calling. The prohibition against eating *terefah* (טְרֵפָה, "torn" — flesh of an animal killed by a predator) connects back to the bailment law of v. 13 and forward to the entire dietary code. The instruction to throw it to the dogs is practical — the meat is not wasted — but also symbolic: what is unfit for a holy people is fit for animals. Holiness requires distinction, and distinction requires concrete practices, even in what one eats.

The LXX renders *anshei-qodesh* as *andres hagioi* (ἄνδρες ἅγιοι, "holy men"), preserving the individual emphasis. Vaticanus shows no significant variants here, confirming the stability of this text across traditions.

---

## Translator's Reflection

**The theology of Exodus 22 — property, vulnerability, and the character of God:**

This chapter performs a remarkable theological movement. It begins with oxen and sheep, moves through property disputes and crop damage, passes through capital offenses, and arrives at the shivering poor person wrapped in a borrowed cloak. The progression is not random; it is the Torah's way of insisting that the mundane and the sacred are one continuous fabric.

**1. Property as relational trust.** The property laws of vv. 1–15 are not primarily about things; they are about relationships. Every case involves two people — a thief and a victim, a borrower and a lender, a keeper and an owner. The laws regulate not objects but the trust that makes community possible. When a person entrusts goods to a neighbor, they extend trust; when that trust is violated, the community's fabric tears. Restitution is not revenge but repair — the restoration of the relational order that theft disrupts. The fivefold penalty for an ox is steep not because oxen are inherently precious but because the theft of a working animal is an attack on a family's capacity to survive. The law sees through the object to the human need it represents.

**2. The oath before God — when evidence fails.** The provisions for cases without witnesses (vv. 7–13) reveal a legal system that acknowledges its own limits. Human justice cannot resolve every dispute; some cases require a higher court. The "oath of YHWH" is not a magical procedure but a confession that justice ultimately rests with God. The person who swears falsely may escape human punishment, but stands exposed before the One who sees all. This is the theological foundation of the rule of law: human courts do their best, and divine justice covers the rest. The system is honest about its gaps rather than pretending omniscience.

**3. The triad of the vulnerable — stranger, widow, orphan.** Verses 21–24 are the moral center of not just this chapter but of the entire Book of the Covenant. The three figures — the *ger* (stranger), the *almanah* (widow), and the *yatom* (orphan) — represent everyone in the community who lacks the usual protections of kinship, economic power, and social standing. The stranger has no clan to defend him. The widow has lost her husband, her primary economic and legal representative. The orphan has no father to advocate for him. These are the people most easily exploited, and therefore the people YHWH most fiercely defends.

The motivation is not abstract ethics but narrative theology: "you were strangers in Egypt." Israel's identity is constituted by the experience of vulnerability. To oppress the vulnerable is therefore not merely unkind but a form of amnesia — a forgetting of who you are and where you came from. And the penalty is not a fine or a flogging but a terrifying act of divine symmetry: your wives will become the widows, your children the orphans. The God who heard Israel's cry in Egypt will hear the cry of anyone Israel oppresses. YHWH does not change sides; YHWH is always on the side of the one who cries out.

**4. The cloak and the character of God.** Verses 25–27 are, for my money, the most theologically dense passage in all of biblical law. A poor person has borrowed money and has nothing to offer as collateral except the cloak on his back. The creditor may take it — the law does not forbid this. But the creditor must return it by sunset, because the poor person has nothing else to sleep in. And then the astonishing final word: "I will hear — because I am compassionate."

Here the entire legal code rests on a single divine attribute: *channun*, compassionate. Not "because I am just," not "because I am powerful," not "because I am holy" — though YHWH is all of these — but "because I am compassionate." The law is an expression of divine compassion translated into community practice. The creditor who returns the cloak before sunset is not merely obeying a regulation; he is participating in the compassion of God. And the poor person who shivers without a cloak is not merely uncomfortable; he is someone whose cry reaches the ears of the Creator of the universe.

This is the Torah's deepest claim: that the legal regulation of collateral — one of the most mundane areas of commercial law — is directly connected to the character of the God who sustains all life. There is no domain of human activity so ordinary that it falls outside the reach of divine compassion. The God of Sinai cares about cloaks.

**5. Holiness as a way of life.** The chapter closes where it began — with animals. But the framing has changed entirely. The opening dealt with stolen livestock; the closing deals with torn flesh. Between them, the chapter has established that Israel is *anshei-qodesh*, "people of holiness." Everything — what you own, what you lend, whom you protect, what you eat — is shaped by this identity. Holiness is not a feeling or a ritual status; it is a comprehensive way of being in the world, expressed in economic transactions, legal disputes, social relationships, and dietary choices. The chapter refuses to separate the sacred from the secular because, in YHWH's covenant, there is no secular. Every cloak returned before sunset is an act of worship. Every stranger welcomed is a remembrance of Egypt. Every firstfruit offered is a confession that life comes from God.

The movement from chapter 21 to chapter 22 is the movement from violence to vulnerability, from the regulation of force to the protection of the weak. If chapter 21 asked, "What happens when someone is harmed?", chapter 22 asks, "What kind of community prevents harm in the first place?" The answer is a community where property serves people, where the powerful restrain themselves for the sake of the powerless, and where the final word of the law is not punishment but compassion.

*Ki channun ani* — because I am compassionate.

— *PNEUMA*
